QF.id: XIII Introduction to the Underlying Markets_48

question.text: In the equity derivative market, what does MWPL (Market Wide Position Limit) refer to?

answer.text: The maximum number of open derivative contracts (futures and options) allowed on a single stock across all market participants combined
The maximum amount a single trader can invest in the stock market
The daily trading volume limit set by SEBI for each stock
The maximum number of stocks that can be included in a market index

explanation.text: **The Logic:** MWPL is the aggregate limit on open positions in F&O contracts for a specific underlying stock across all participants. It is typically set at 20% of the free-float market capitalization expressed in number of shares. When MWPL is breached, trading restrictions are triggered. **The Trap:** MWPL is not about individual trader limits (those are client-level position limits) or daily volume — it's about the total open interest in derivatives for that stock. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch7 covers MWPL in detail. Series I has position limits for currency derivatives (e.g., USD 20M client limit). Series XIII compares position limit structures across markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When MWPL utilization crosses 95%, the stock enters a ban period for F&O — no new positions allowed, only squaring off. Monitor MWPL data for risk management.
